articles.title: Understanding "Handoffs" in LangChain(One Agent, Many Personalities)
articles.excerpt: If you've been building with LangChain agents, you've probably run into a situation like this: you want your agent to behave differently depending on where the conversation is. Maybe it should ask cla…
articles.body: If you've been building with LangChain agents, you've probably run into a situation like this: you want your agent to behave differently depending on where the conversation is. Maybe it should ask clarifying questions at first, then switch to solving the problem, then wrap things up politely. The tempting solution is to build three separate agents and pass the conversation between them. But there's a simpler pattern that gets you the same result with a single agent: handoffs . In this post, I'll walk through a small support-bot example that uses this pattern, and explain what's actually happening under the hood. What is a "handoff," really? A handoff is not a new agent taking over. It's the same agent , but its instructions and available tools change based on a piece of state.
articles.author: Syeed Talha

articles.title: Your Website’s New Audience: AI Agents
articles.excerpt: Web performance has traditionally always been about human user experience. In recent time, that has evolved to include AI agents as well. AI agents can be thought of as anonymous and agentic systems t…
articles.body: Web performance has traditionally always been about human user experience. In recent time, that has evolved to include AI agents as well. AI agents can be thought of as anonymous and agentic systems that browse, consume and interact the web on behalf of users. As more and more people continue to use AI agents or search engines pushing AI mode, it is recommended to optimize for agentic browsing as well. Google has recently included an agentic browsing category for both lighthouse and pagespeed insights, although they are currently experimental. How do we do that? Broadly speaking, there are a few basic steps that can be covered here: 1. Accessibility Tree: When agents browse your page, they do not typically "see" the page as a human would.
articles.author: Shwetha

articles.title: Why is most trading psychology advice useless? | Lumir S Vinod
articles.excerpt: To read the original version, click here. It’s very easy to say, “Be patient” or “Money management is more important than trading strategies.” That’s generic advice. What they fail to explain is why p…
articles.body: To read the original version, click here. Most of it screams about money management and patience. But do you know what the problem is? It’s very easy to say, “Be patient” or “Money management is more important than trading strategies.” That’s generic advice. What they fail to explain is why people can’t manage money or why they struggle to be patient. The answer lies in psychology. Addressing those underlying psychological reasons can help traders improve or at least become more aware the next time they’re in that situation. But many of these so called advisors don’t do that. They’re chasing views, not the quality of the content.
articles.author: Lumir S Vinod

articles.title: Avoir une apk ou aab ? Facile.
articles.excerpt: PhilBuilder : Compiler le Web en Natif, Gratuitement L'alternative que l'Afrique (et le reste du monde) n'attendait plus Le problème est simple. Tu codes en HTML, CSS, JavaScript. Tu as une idée, un p…
articles.body: PhilBuilder : Compiler le Web en Natif, Gratuitement L'alternative que l'Afrique (et le reste du monde) n'attendait plus Le problème est simple. Tu codes en HTML, CSS, JavaScript. Tu as une idée, un projet, un prototype. Tu veux le transformer en application Android installable — un vrai APK, signé, prêt pour le Play Store. Tu cherches un service en ligne qui le fasse pour toi. Et là, tu tombes sur les murs. VoltBuilder, Ionic Appflow, les solutions existantes fonctionnent. Mais elles coûtent. 20, 50, 100 dollars par mois pour encapsuler du web dans un shell natif. Pour un lycéen en Côte d'Ivoire, un étudiant au Sénégal, un développeur solo n'importe où, c'est une barrière. Ce n'est pas un prix, c'est une sélection. J'ai refusé cette sélection.
articles.author: Henock Agbo

articles.title: How I Gave My AI Agent a Crypto Wallet in 30 Minutes (And You Can Too)
articles.excerpt: How I Gave My AI Agent a Crypto Wallet in 30 Minutes (And You Can Too) AI agents are smart, but they're broke. They can research, code, and solve problems — but they can't pay for anything. No credit…
articles.body: How I Gave My AI Agent a Crypto Wallet in 30 Minutes (And You Can Too) AI agents are smart, but they're broke. They can research, code, and solve problems — but they can't pay for anything. No credit card. No bank account. No way to hold value. Here's how I fixed that for my agent in under 30 minutes with $10 and an API key. Why Crypto Wallets for AI? Traditional finance requires legal personhood. Banks need IDs, SSNs, and physical addresses. Software doesn't have any of that. Blockchain solves this. A crypto wallet is just a public-private key pair — no identity required.

articles.title: Rick and Morty Season 9, Episode 9 Review & Recap: "Salute Your Morts"
articles.excerpt: Rick and Morty Season 9's penultimate episode is a wacky romp, one that finally turns the spotlight to the oft-ignored Beth. Read our full review.
articles.body: Warning: This review contains full spoilers for Rick and Morty Season 9, Episode 9! I think most of us would agree that Rick and Morty Season 9 was way overdue for a Beth-centric episode. After being relegated to the background for eight episodes, things finally change in the penultimate chapter of Season 9. "Salute Your Morts" is an overall fun romp, but it really excels when it comes to exploring how Beth (Sarah Chalke) and Jerry (Chris Parnell) deal with the curse of eternal intoxication. "Salute Your Morts" benefits from a solid and very sensible premise.

articles.title: House of the Dragon: Why Did Vhagar Abandon Aemond?
articles.excerpt: One of the most curious events in this episode is the disappearance of the dragon Vhagar, who apparently abandoned her ailing rider Aemond at Harrenhal to go on a countryside spree while he recovers f…
articles.body: Spoilers follow for both House of the Dragon and Fire & Blood. A lot happened in House of the Dragon: Season 3, Episode 5 , from unseen assassins creating a gruesome tableaux of corpses worthy of a Thomas Harris serial killer to Alicent and Helaena’s escape plan ending in a cliffhanger. One of the most curious events in this episode is the disappearance of the dragon Vhagar, who apparently abandoned her ailing rider Aemond at Harrenhal to go on a countryside spree while he recovers from his stab wound in the care of the enigmatic Alys Rivers. But as Aemond, as out of it as he is, correctly noted, Vhagar would never just leave him.
articles.author: Jim Vejvoda

articles.title: The Vampire Lestat Showrunner Reveals Hidden Meanings and Future Stories Hinted at in the Season 3 Finale
articles.excerpt: The Vampire Lestat showrunner Rolin Jones unpacks the parallel journeys of Louis and Lestat… as well as that massive cliffhanger in the season finale, "The Failures."
articles.body: Spoilers follow for The Vampire Lestat Season 3, Episode 7 - “The Failures” which is available on AMC and AMC+ now. While Season 3 of AMC’s The Vampire Lestat (formerly known as Anne Rice's Interview With the Vampire ) was promoted through the lens of Lestat de Lioncourt’s “ROCK STAR!” image, in truth the season was really about exposing 200 years worth of vulnerabilities festering within this mercurial bloodsucker (played by Sam Reid). In this season’s finale episode, “The Failures,” a beheaded Lestat mulls his multitude of sins as they come back to haunt him in a mind palace of his own making.
articles.author: Scott Collura

articles.title: House of the Dragon season 3 episode 5 explained: Who’s killing the Gold Cloaks?
articles.excerpt: The latest threat to Rhaenyra Targaryen's reign is a string of grisly murders targeting the city watch. And the mystery gets very dark.
articles.body: Every time queen Rhaenyra Targaryen (Emma D’Arcy) has a council meeting with her advisors, they spend most of the meeting bickering about what the realm’s biggest problems are these days. Is it the crown’s lack of funding? The lingering threat of Aemond (Ewan Mitchell) and his dragon Vhagar? Ormund Hightower (James Norton)’s hostile takeover of Tumbleton? Or civil unrest amongst the smallfolk? In House of the Dragon ’s latest episode, Daemon Targaryen (Matt Smith) brings yet another big problem to the council, and it’s one that hits very close to home: Somebody is killing Gold Cloaks, members of the city guard. But who?
articles.author: Corey Plante

articles.title: House of the Dragon Season 3, Episode 5 Recap and Review
articles.excerpt: House of the Dragon: Season 3 continues to fire on all cylinders – or dragons, possibly. Even as viewers we can see the forces aligning against Rhaenyra and the pitfalls that surround her, and yet she…
articles.body: Full spoilers follow for House of the Dragon Season 3, Episode 5. Is Rhaenyra paranoid, or is someone really out to get her? Well, definitely the latter – but she might also be paranoid, and that’s the problem. There is plotting on all sides: some relatively benign, and some potentially lethal, and it’s hard to tell where the real dangers are coming from. Things continue tense, in other words, and that is keeping this show as gripping as Game Of Thrones ever was. We open on a frustrated Lord Oscar Tully (Archie Barnes), whose attempts to get his Rivermen army to Tumbleton have been stymied by Christen Cole (Fabian Frankel) and his guerilla warriors burning bridges along the route. Cole attacks the Rivermen camp that night, but that’s as good as it gets for him.
articles.author: Jim Vejvoda

articles.title: House of the Dragon finally solves a political mystery that Game of Thrones never did
articles.excerpt: The latest House of the Dragon episode revealed why House Martell of Dorne might be the answer to the destruction of the Targaryens
articles.body: George R.R. Martin ’s fantasy books set in the world of Westeros are full of political intrigue, and HBO has brought that to life with relative ease in Game of Thrones , House of the Dragon , and even in the more commoner-oriented TV series, A Knight of the Seven Kingdoms . From Aegon’s Conquest of Westeros to Robert’s Rebellion against the Targaryen dynasty, the fragile alliances and endless scheming that hold Westeros together are a big part of what keeps audiences coming back for more, and obsessing over the story in between new seasons.
articles.author: Aimee Hart

articles.title: 'Aliens' at 40: James Cameron's sequel is a sci-fi icon, but did you know he quit the movie twice?
articles.excerpt: Forty years ago, Aliens burst into our hearts, but this classic sequel nearly fell by the wayside due to some serious studio-created problems that saw James Cameron quit twice.
articles.body: Where does James Cameron's "Aliens" rank among the other Alien movies ? That depends on who you ask. Some might say it's the best, while others put it just behind the original (spoiler alert: it's the best). Regardless, there's no denying that this 1986 film shifted gears, moving the franchise from horror to horror-tinged sci-fi action and paving an alternate path for the future. As Cameron stated in an interview with Lofficier , the plan always was "to do a film that was not as scary... it's scary, but it's not as scary, but more intense, and I like to use the word, exhilarating. Because I think you get exhilarated by the intensity of the kind of action that's in this film." In terms of story progression, the sequel's shake-up makes complete sense.
articles.author: Sergio Pereira

articles.title: Should we trust AI in the search for alien life? Scientists aren't so sure
articles.excerpt: New research suggests AI can be repeatedly fooled into mistaking non-life for life.
articles.body: Artificial intelligence could easily fall into the trap of identifying non-life as life on other worlds, claim two researchers from Michigan State University who have tested AI on simulated life in a computer program. "We had previously seen that AI has a big Achilles heel when it is trying to classify things that are unlike the things in its training examples," Michigan's Christoph Adami told Space.com. "We call these 'out-of-distribution' samples and it is just incredibly easy to get AI to misclassify." Adami is a computational biologist who uses computers to apply information theory to the study of evolution and biology. One of his leading tenets is that life can be defined by its ability to encode information and replicate it.
articles.author: Keith Cooper

articles.title: How Christopher Nolan's 'Interstellar' turned general relativity into the bad guy
articles.excerpt: As Nolan embarks on "The Odyssey", we look back on the sci-fi movie where time dilation is as cruel as any Greek god.
articles.body: The biggest blockbusters of 2014 threw up a varied rogues' gallery of bad guys. In "Transformers: Age of Extinction", it was a reincarnated version of Optimus Prime's perma-nemesis, Megatron, while Captain America faced off against a reborn Hydra infiltrating the upper echelons of the US government in "The Winter Soldier". For Spider-Man, it was Electro, while Maleficent showed up in live-action to give Sleeping Beauty nightmares. Christopher Nolan has never been one to go with the Hollywood crowd, however, and in his outer space opus "Interstellar", the antagonist was time itself — or, to be more specific, Einstein's theory of general relativity. Nolan, whose Homeric epic "The Odyssey" makes landfall in theaters this week, has always been obsessed with time, and our perception of it.
articles.author: Richard Edwards

articles.title: UAC-0145 Uses ClickFix CAPTCHAs to Infect Ukrainian Devices wih Malware

articles.title: 10 places in the US and Canada to see August's partial solar eclipse and Perseid meteor shower in 1 trip
articles.excerpt: A partial solar eclipse by day and the peak of the Perseid meteor shower by night make Aug. 12, 2026, one of the best nights for skywatching and camping in years, but only for some parts of North Amer…
articles.body: Aug. 12, 2026, offers a rare double-header for skywatchers across northeastern North America: a partial solar eclipse during the afternoon followed, hours later, by the peak of the Perseid meteor shower beneath dark summer skies. The eclipse is part of a total solar eclipse happening in eastern Greenland, western Iceland and northern Spain. "The neat thing about seeing a partial eclipse, especially one that is total somewhere else, is that you get to join in with the whole event that's going on," Tyler Nordgren, astronomer and eclipse artist at Space Art Travel Bureau , told Space.com.
articles.author: Jamie Carter

articles.title: James Webb Space Telescope discovers how black holes feed themselves
articles.excerpt: Using the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), astronomers have gotten a glimpse at the mechanisms that supermassive black holes use to feed themselves.
articles.body: Thanks to the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST), astronomers have been given a glimpse of the mechanisms that supermassive black holes use to feed themselves. The observations could help scientists discover how supermassive black holes with masses millions, often even billions, of times that of the sun grew so fast so soon after the Big Bang. While all large galaxies have supermassive black holes at their hearts, some are slumbering giants, consuming little surrounding gas and dust, while others are ravenously feeding and acting as the engines of bright central galactic regions called active galactic nuclei (AGN).
articles.author: Robert Lea

articles.title: Scientists told them, 'No, it's too dangerous,' but they did it anyway: Inside Japan's super-close asteroid flyby
articles.excerpt: Japan's Hayabusa2 spacecraft returned spectacular images of a near-Earth asteroid following a super-close flyby on July 5, but heated debate was needed before teams signed off on the daring attempt.
articles.body: Japan's Hayabusa2 spacecraft returned spectacular images of a near-Earth asteroid following a super-close flyby on July 5, but heated debate was needed before teams signed off on the daring attempt. When images of the asteroid Torifune arrived on the morning of July 6 Japan time, Makoto Yoshikawa and his team at the Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency ( JAXA ) got two surprises at once. Torifune turned out to be a contact binary, in which two chunks of rock have come together under gravity, and the returned images were also larger than hoped. "We did not imagine such a contact binary ," Yoshikawa, former mission manager of Hayabusa2 , told scientists gathered at the Asteroids, Comets and Meteors conference in Poznan, Poland, on July 10.
articles.author: Andrew Jones
